title: A STEPPED SAMPLING METHOD FOR VIDEO DETECTION USING LSTM [PDF]

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## A STEPPED SAMPLING METHOD FOR VIDEO DETECTION USING LSTM

## **Anonymous authors Paper under double-blind review**

## ABSTRACT

Artificial neural networks are considered to simulate the human neural networks, and achieves great progress on object detection, natural language processing (NLP), image generation, etc. Hermann Ebbinghaus proposed the law of human memory and how to improve human learning in 1885. Inspiring from Ebbinghaus’ work, we propose a stepped sampler based on the “repeated input”, which is Ebbinghaus’ contribution that how to strengthen the learning. We repeatedly inputted data to the LSTM model stepwise in a batch. The stepped sampler is used to strengthen the ability of fusing the temporal information in LSTM. We tested the stepped sampler on the LSTM offered by PyTorch. Compared with the traditional sampler of PyTorch, such as sequential sampler, batch sampler, the training loss of the proposed stepped sampler converges faster in the model training, and the training loss after convergence is more stable, which means that there is no large jitter after the convergence. Meanwhile, it can maintain a higher test accuracy, compared with the traditional sampler. We quantified the algorithm of the stepped sampler. We assume that, the artificial neural networks may have human-like characteristics, and human learning method could be used for machine learning. Our code will be available online soon.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The emergence of convolutional neural networks (CNN) (LeCun et al., 1989) has improved the selflearning ability of artificial neural networks. Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) (Mikolov et al., 2010) is used to process the temporal information data. RNN takes the output of the previous time period as the input of the next time period, effectively using the temporal information of the input sequence.

RNN sometimes may have the problem of gradient disappearance or gradient explosion. Hochreiter et al. (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) proposed LSTM. LSTM adds gates to RNN, thus it can effectively avoid the problem of gradient disappearance or explosion. These gates include the forgetting gates, the input gates, and the output gates. The forgetting gate seems to be the most important among them. LSTM may simulate the memory process of human brain. Human brain selectively forgets some information for learning better.

Consider that one of the principles of neural networks may be learned from biological neural networks, for those artificial neural networks with the memory effects, such as LSTM, learning from the memory method of human, which is the repeated input and timely review, we study the effect of this method with repeated input on LSTM detection results, without considering changing the LSTM network structure.

In this study, we learn the effect of the proposed input method on neural networks with memory characteristics, such as LSTM. Specifically, it is to repeatedly input training data by simulating the “repeated input and timely review” method of the human memory, and the “repeated input and timely review” method is proposed by Hermann Ebbinghaus (Ebbinghaus, 1913) in 1885, which is the “Increasing Memory Strength and Rate of Learning” in his literature.

## 1.1 OUR CONTRIBUTION

Our views in this paper mainly include the following 3 aspects:

**a)** A novel sampler is proposed, which implements sampling in a circular and stepwise manner. Compared with the traditional sampler, the loss curve of the LSTM model using this stepped sampler converges faster in training, and is more stable after the convergence, namely there is no large jitter after the convergence. Moreover, its test accuracy curve is more stable either, which has no jitter. When the batch size is 15, the test accuracy of the stepped sampler LSTM is much higher than that of the traditional sampler with the same parameters.

**b)** The idea of this sampler comes from the laws of human memory, which was proposed by Ebbinghaus (Ebbinghaus, 1913). We courageously assume that, other human learning methods can also be applied to machine learning. One example is the proposal of the attention mechanism (Vaswani et al., 2017). Moreover, we believe that artificial neural networks have human-like characteristics from the experimental performance.

**c)** We try to use mathematical language to describe the temporal information of the video frames. We try to apply the mathematical equations to our experimental results, and analyze that the test accuracy in the experiment is the temporal information between video frames. The derivation process is shown in Appendix A and Appendix B.

## 2 RELATED WORK

Gibbs sampling is one of the earlier data sampling algorithms, which is proposed by Geman et al. (Geman & Geman, 1984) in 1984. Gibbs sampling is to make the probability of the data sample approximately equal to the required probability distribution via iterations. Gibbs sampling randomly selects data from an initial input sequence, and iterates according to the specified conditional probabilities, which are related to the required probability distribution of the final sampling data. After iterations, Gibbs sampling generates data which is consistent with the required probability distribution. Hu et al. (Hu et al., 2018) used neural networks to generate a sampler, which transfer the initial data distribution to the target distribution. The method can generate the sampling data at the same time of training. This method works with the un-normalized probability density function. Wang et al. (Wang et al., 2018) used Generative Adversarial Nets (GAN) (Goodfellow et al., 2014) to generate the negative samples. The approach is the first to combine GAN with the negative sampling method, which improves the training effect of the streaming recommend system. Chu et al. (Chu et al., 2019) proposed a novel sampler that can sample both the positive and the negative data from the input data sequences, so as to let the classifier utilize the Regions of Interests and the background of the data. The sampler is used in the few-shot image classifier, which uses the reinforcement learning method. The reinforcement learning algorithm (Kaelbling et al., 1996) needs to continuously select the regions of interests from the images, subsequently to recognize the content of the Regions of Interests. Sampling these Regions of Interests can improve the efficiency of reinforcement learning, for the reason of the reduction of the input samples. Muhammad et al. (Muhammad et al., 2021) proposed a bi-directional long short-term memory (BiLSTM) with attention mechanism and a dilated convolutional neural network (DCNN) to perform action recognition, which outperformed the state-of-the-art methods. Kwon et al. (Kwon et al., 2021) proposed a spatio-temporal neighbourhood learning method on action recognition, which performed the state-of-the-art.

## 3 MATERIALS AND METHODS

This paper is from the perspective of data input, rather than the neural network structure, and study the impact of the memory effect on the temporal sequence neural networks (such as LSTM). The process simulates the method of enhancing the memory process of human brain, repeats the input data in a stepped way. The method is proposed by Hermann Ebbinghaus (Ebbinghaus, 1913) called “Increasing rate of learning” in his book. The specific mode we used was the wheel tactic (Smith, 1994) when we recited words, by establishing a novel data sampler in the LSTM model training. The dataset in the experiment is UCF101 (Soomro et al., 2012), which is a human action recognition video dataset. The name of each folder indicates the annotation of the video.

## 3.1 EBBINGHAUS FORGETTING CURVE

Ebbinghaus forgetting curve (Ebbinghaus, 1913) describes the memory effect of human brain over time, which was proposed by Hermann Ebbinghaus in 1885. This theory reveals the human memory law. It is also the law of human learning. That is, the loss of human memory when learning new knowledge is drop fast first and slow later. Ebbinghaus also pointed out that, timely review and repeated input are the key point to prevent forgetting, consolidate knowledge, and learn better. Figure 1 illustrates Ebbinghaus forgetting curve, and timely review can reduce the knowledge forgetting, which makes the learning better. Based on Ebbinghaus forgetting curve on the human brain, we simulated Ebbinghaus’ method on machine learning. We believe that the experimental results in Section 4 could prove that there is a certain correlation between human learning and machine learning, since the machine learning method with timely review and spaced repeat has a faster learning effect, compared with the machine learning without the human-like method.

Ebbinghaus also found that, making good use of the correlations between knowledge is another key point for enhancing learning. We definite these correlations are temporal information in Appendix A. Thereby enhancing the use of temporal information is the key to video detection, natural language processing (NLP), etc. We believe that, the partly repeated input of the stepped sampler enhances the correlation and the temporal information.

## 3.2 LSTM

The LSTM architecture we used in this paper is to start with a CNN backbone. The CNN backbone has four convolutional layers. The dimension of convolution kernels of each convolutional layer is 32, 64, 128, 256. The size of each convolution kernel is 5 _×_ 5 _,_ 3 _×_ 3 _,_ 3 _×_ 3 _,_ 3 _×_ 3, the stride of the convolution kernel is 2, and the padding is 0. Each convolutional layer is followed by a batch normalization (BN) (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015) layer and a ReLU layer. The last part of the CNN model is 3 fully connected (FC) layers, which use dropout function. The dimensions of the 3 FC layers are 1024, 768 and 512 respective-

## 3.3 THE STEPPED SAMPLER

Our experiment is compared with the common sampler. Common sampler in PyTorch (Paszke et al., 2019) include random sampler, weighted random sampler, batch sampler, etc. Batch sampling is nearly the most commonly used. The previous research is to add memory units to deep learning networks, such as RNN, LSTM, etc. Analogous to human learning, an important point is repetition. And the sampler should be an appropriate way to simulate the “repetition”, since the data in each batch can be designed to be input repeatedly. We suppose that, “repetition” is important, not only for human beings, but also for computers. To make computers better use the “repetition”, analogizing the way how we recite words, we propose a “stepped” repetition input method, which is the stepped sampler.

The structure of the proposed stepped sampler is illustrated in Figure 2. It is established on the batch sampler. The stepped sampler divides a batch into some sub-batches. Like human memory, this sampler adopts the principle of adjacent repetition (Crowder, 1968), namely, the back of the previous sub-batch is the same with the front of the next sub-batch.

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

The structure of the stepped sampler shows that, the input data of different batches is partly duplicated. The repeated input seems to increase the redundancy, but the experimental results show that, with our experimental environment, this method can accelerate the convergence of LSTM model. There is a stride between the previous sub-batch and the next sub-batch. The stride size n can be set manually. We believe that this part repetition enhances the correlation of the input frames, thereby enhancing the temporal information of the input frames, according to our definition of the temporal information in Appendix A. Section 4 describes the comparative experiments on the sampler with different stride size.

## 3.4 THE ALGORITHM OF THE STEPPED SAMPLER

The stepped sampler is designed on the basis of batch sampler. The algorithm is designed to implement stepped sampling within each batch via the batch sampler. The workflow of the algorithm is as follows: the data first goes through the sequential sampler of PyTorch, then, they are processed to batches by the batch sampler. Finally, the data in each batch are divided into sub-batches with the same strides by the stepped sampler.

The algorithm of the proposed sampler is shown in Algorithm 1. The idea is to implement the stepped sampler in each batch after the sequential sampler and batch sampler of PyTorch. Line 12 of Algorithm 1 is that, after each previous stepped sub-batchs output, the starting coordinate is moved by n (step stride) data from the starting position of the previous sub-batch.

## 4 RESULTS

## 4.1 EXPERIMENT SETUP

The system used in the experiment was a workstation with 32 GB CPU RAM and a NVIDIA GeForce 1080ti GPU. The processor was Intel i7 8700, the operating system was Ubuntu 16.04 64 bits. PyTorch version used in the experiment was 1.0.1, Python version was 3.6, Numpy version was 1.20.4, Sklearn version was 0.20.4, and Matplotlib, Pandas, tqdm were implemented as the software environment. The reason why we chose old version PyTorch is, the performance of the old version may not be so powelful, however, the experimental effect may be better, since the contrast of the results may be larger. And since the old version may not have too many functions, it can focus on the factor of “repeat input”, without the interferences from other irrelevant factors.

We searched the relevant literature, and found that there may be no LSTM literature that applies human learning methods to machine learning for now. Therefore, the experiment we designed is a comparison experiment, which is an ordinary CNN-LSTM, with or without the stepped sampler, and the other parameters are all the same. One of the advantages of this is that, it can reduce the influence of other irrelevant factors, and can specifically concentrate on the machine learning results of human learning methods. And the human learning methods is the timely review proposed by Ebbinghaus.

## 4.2 TRAINING

The detection accuracy evaluation and cross-entropy loss were used for the training of the models. The accuracy evaluation in the experiment used the accuracy score tool in the Sklearn package of Python. The cross-entropy loss used the function of PyTorch. The accuracy and loss were graphically depicted in Figure 4, 5 and 6. The accuracy and loss were computed every epoch. The dataset of

4

UCF101 was split into training set and test set by a ratio of 3:1. After training, an overall accuracy and loss were computed by the test set, to evaluate the performance of the models. The epoch was set to 150. We used Adam as the optimization algorithm. We experimented different batch sizes and step sizes, which was changing the size of L, m and the step stride n shown in Figure 2.

Our experiment is trained from scratch. Training from scratch may decrease the test accuracy, but it can eliminate interference and focus on the stepped sampler. The learning rate was set to 0.0001. The momentum was set to 0.01. The operators of batch normalization (BN) and ReLU activation were used after each convolutional layer in the CNN backbone. The CNN backbone is not shown in Figure 2. The data transformation was applied to enhance the network. The input frames were transformed into 256 _×_ 342 pixels.

## 4.3 EXPERIMENT RESULTS

Figure 3 are the visualized results.We tested the sampler of batch size 25. Figure 4 presents the experimental results. Each subfigure shows the training loss and test accuracy of the models. The difference of the models is only the sampler, for comparing the results only caused by the sampler. Figure 4 (a) is the model of traditional sampler, which is the sequential sampler and the batch sampler in PyTorch, and the other in Figure 4 are the models of the proposed stepped sampler. Figure 4 (b), (c), (d), (e), (f) are only different from the step stride for comparing. From Figure 4, we consider that step stride 2 (batch size 25, step size 20, in Figure 4 (c)) is the optimal. The training loss in Figure 4 (a) has many jitters, even when the epoch is more than 110, while the training loss in the other subfigures are much smoother, and can converge earlier than the traditional batch sampler model (Figure 4 (a)). Nonetheless, the test accuracy score of the traditional batch sampler model (Figure 4 (a)) is slightly higher. The test accuracy of Figure 4 (a) can be 0.656, while the test accuracy of the model with stride 2 stepped sampler (Figure 4 (c)) can be 0.603. The test accuracy of the models in Figure 4 is shown in Table 1.

From Figure 4, the following could be concluded: **a)** In the model training, LSTM with the stepped sampler converges faster than LSTM with the traditional sampler, and the convergence effect is better, i.e., there is no large jitter after the drop. **b)** When the batch size and the step size are fixed, the smaller the step stride was, the worse the detection effect became. Similarly, the larger the step stride was, the worse the detection effect became either. If the batch size and the step size are fixed, the detection effect seems to be a normal distribution of the step stride. **c)** However, LSTM with the traditional sampler whose batch size is 25 has a higher test accuracy on the test set. Although this value is not much higher than the optimal stepped sampler model (Figure 4 (c)).

From Table 1, it can be concluded that, for the same batch size, the test accuracy of the LSTM with stepped sampler rises faster than the traditional sampler LSTM. This can also be seen in Figure 4. Figure 5 and Figure 6 are the illustration of the traditional LSTM and the stepped sampler LSTM, when the batch sizes are all set to 20,15 respectively. The training loss of Figure 5 (c) and Figure 6 (c) converge faster than Figure 5 (a) and Figure 6 (a) , which denotes that, our method may have a broad-spectrum effect on machine learning. The test accuracy score of Figure 6 (c) is higher than Figure 6 (a), which denotes that, the stepped sampler LSTM may have higher test accuracy than the traditional sampler LSTM, when the batch size is 15, step size is 10, step stride is 5 of the stepped sampler LSTM, and the traditional sampler LSTM is with batch size 15. It can be seen that, there is a large jitter when the epoch is about 100 in Figure 6 (a). Figure 6 (b) and Figure 6 (c) have no large jitter after the epoch is about 60. The training loss of Figure 6 (c) drops faster than Figure 6 (a). The test accuracy of the three models is shown in Table 2. From Figure 6 we can see that, the training loss of the stepped sampler model still converges faster than the traditional sampler model.

In our experiments, most LSTM models with the stepped sampler have a more stable convergence of training loss, compared with the traditional LSTM models with the same batch size. Figure 4 (a) and Figure 6 (a) are the loss curves of the traditional batch sampler, it can be seen that, the loss curves have large jitters after the convergence. Other loss curves in Figure 4 and Figure 6 are stabler after the convergence. The stepped sampler LSTM may have a higher test accuracy than the traditional sampler LSTM, in the same batch size, which can reach the value of 0.639 (Table 2).

Our test uses the shuffle operation. ShuffleNet (Zhang et al., 2018) proves that the shuffle operation can improve the image detection mAP. We analyse that, the reason should be that the shuffle operation can reduce the correlation. According to our definition in Appendix A, the correlation

is the temporal information. Therefore, we consider that the shuffle operation can reduce the temporal information. If the shuffle operation is not used during detection, the frames are sequential. We believe that this continuity will have a certain impact on the model with temporal information. Since the test data is shuffled, there should be less temporal information among the data, the test results may reflect the detection effect better. The literature (Zhou et al., 2018) proves that, shuffle operation makes little impact on UCF101, and we think there would be no disadvantages for us to use the shuffle operation when testing.

## 4.4 THE TRAINING TIME

A batch is divided into multiple sub-batches might prolong the training time. However, since the repeated data are the same, the training time of a sub-batch is much shorter than an ordinary batch. Therefore, for the total training time, the stepped sampler and the traditional sampler are almost the same. For example, when the batch sizes are all set to 15, the stepped sampler with the step stride of 5 and the traditional batch sampler both take about 60 hours for training with our experimental conditions. Moreover, we believe that the training epoch which the stepped sampler needs might be less than that of the traditional sampler.

## 5 DISCUSSION

The experiment is to study the detection effect of the proposed sampler, which simulates one of the human brain memory law, repeating the input, to use the temporal information (we think it is the correlation in/between frames) of videos.

As the data are partly repeated inputted, it may be equivalent to the timely knowledge review of human brain, which strengthens the memory of the LSTM network, and reduces the information forgetting. The process is very similar with human learning, which was revealed by Hermann Ebbinghaus, and it is illustrated in Subsection 3.1. LSTM can selectively memorize the temporal information, which is human-like.

From Figure 4, the repeating times of the stepped sampler is not the more the better, as shown in Figure 4 (b), the stride is 1, and the convergence speed of the model is not improved much. In addition, the repeating times of the stepped sampler is not the less the better. As shown in Figure 4 (f), the convergence speed of the model is even slower. Figure 5 (b) also seems to show this. The phenomenon is the same with human learning. Too much repetitive input and too little repetitive input would not improve the learning effect of human. The experiments seem to verify the similarity between machine learning and human learning. We assume that artificial neural networks have human-like characteristics. What is the best spaced repetition, is still need to be studied. We assume that, it should be “one solution to one issue”, just like human.

Temporal information also seems to have human-like characteristics. Temporal information is the correlation of temporal sequences, analogous to human learning, it is the correlation of knowledge. In human learning, one of the important learning methods is to use the correlation of knowledge, and using the temporal information may also be one of the important learning methods of machine learning.

## 6 CONCLUSION

We refer to the human memory rules, and propose the stepped sampler, a repeating input method which uses the timely review approach. The timely review approach was proposed by Ebbinghaus, and is used to strengthen human memory and learning. In our experiments, this method has a better promotion on the detection effect of LSTM. The experimental results show that, compared with the traditional sampler, the training loss of the stepped sampler converges faster, and is more stable after the convergence, i.e., there is no large jitter after convergence. The test accuracy of the model with the stepped sampler also reaches a high point faster and is more stable either. When the batch size is 15, the test accuracy of the stepped sampler LSTM is significantly higher than that of the traditional sampler with the same batch size. We analyzed the algorithm of stepped sampler and got several equations. Ebbinghaus also pointed out that utilizing the correlations between knowledge is another key to learn better. We believe that this part repetition of the sampler enhances the correlation of the input frames, thereby enhancing the temporal information of the input frames, from our definition of the temporal information in Appendix A.

We try to use the mathematical language to describe the temporal information of video frames, which is shown in Appendix A. Since these mathematical descriptions do not involve specific artificial neural networks, the parameters of neural network are not added to the equations.

We try to use some human learning methods to study artificial neural networks. Compared with the traditional sampler, the stepped sampler LSTM has a faster learning effect, and has a higher test accuracy under certain parameters. The results show that, there may be a close relationship between biological neural network and artificial neural network, whatever in structure and even in principle. How to improve human learning is different for each individual, and the test accuracy of our experiment may illustrate this point, and we believe that is why not all the stepped sampler LSTM’s test accuracy is higher than the traditional sampler. The attention mechanism (Vaswani et al., 2017) may be also inspired by human learning methods. Transfer learning (Bozinovski & Fulgosi, 1976) is using old knowledge to learn new knowledge, which may be inspired by human learning methods either. We believe that, artificial neural networks seem to have human-like characteristics, and human learning and machine learning seem to have some similarities.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents an ambitious and conceptually novel idea—drawing inspiration from Ebbinghaus’ psychological principles of human memory to improve training dynamics in LSTM-based video detection through a proposed “stepped sampler.” While the work raises intriguing interdisciplinary connections between cognitive science and machine learning, its current form suffers from significant issues that undermine its scientific rigor, clarity, and technical contribution. The central claim—that repeatedly inputting data in a stepped, cyclical manner improves convergence speed, stability, and test accuracy—is inadequately supported by evidence: no formal experimental setup, baselines, or statistical comparisons are provided in the excerpts, and key elements such as model architecture, training protocol, evaluation metrics, and dataset preprocessing are either omitted or under-specified. The reference to “Appendix A and B” for mathematical derivation hints at analytical justification that is not accessible in this excerpt, leaving the core technical contribution unclear. Furthermore, the writing is often imprecise, with vague terminology (e.g., “repeated input and timely review” applied analogously to data sampling), unsupported claims (e.g., "artificial neural networks have human-like characteristics"), and a lack of alignment with standard machine learning methodology. The connection to Ebbinghaus’ work appears superficial without a mechanistic or theoretical bridge between human memory consolidation and data sampling strategies in deep learning. Given these concerns, while the high-level vision is creative, the paper currently lacks the clarity, empirical grounding, and technical depth expected for a top-tier conference like ICLR. Substantial revisions—particularly in formalizing the method, providing rigorous ablation studies, clarifying the novelty with respect to existing sampling or curriculum learning techniques, and improving exposition—are necessary to establish the proposed approach as a credible contribution.

comments.title: Inconsistent and overstated claim about stepped sampler's test accuracy superiority
comments.quote: Meanwhile, it can maintain a higher test accuracy, compared with the traditional sampler.
comments.explanation: The abstract and multiple sections (e.g., Section 1.1, 4.3) assert that the stepped sampler achieves higher test accuracy, but results contradict this claim. At batch size 25, the traditional sampler achieves higher test accuracy (0.656 vs. 0.603 for stride 2), yet the paper frames the stepped sampler as generally superior without sufficient qualification. While improved accuracy is observed at smaller batch sizes (e.g., 15), this is presented selectively. Furthermore, final performance at epoch 150 shows the traditional sampler at 0.635 versus 0.593 for the best stepped variant, contradicting the initial 0.656 claim — suggesting possible misreporting. Table 1 and text values conflict (e.g., 0.656 appears only at epoch 100), undermining trust. The claim is overgeneralized and not consistently supported, risking misrepresentation of the method’s benefit.

comments.title: Contradictory evaluation setup: shuffling test data undermines temporal modeling claims
comments.quote: We analyse that, the reason should be that the shuffle operation can reduce the correlation. According to our definition in Appendix A, the correlation is the temporal information. Therefore, we consider that the shuffle operation can reduce the temporal information.
comments.explanation: The authors claim their stepped sampler improves temporal modeling in LSTMs by preserving sequence continuity, yet they shuffle test data and argue this leads to 'better' evaluation by reducing temporal information. This contradicts their core premise: if temporal structure is essential, shuffling should degrade performance and weaken the test of their method. Shuffling across video samples does not eliminate intra-sequence temporal dependencies, but the justification that it 'reduces temporal information' and thereby improves evaluation logic implies such structure is undesirable — which undercuts the motivation for stepped sampling. The reasoning conflates inter-sample shuffling with destruction of intra-video order and risks invalidating the experimental assessment of their own method. Citations to ShuffleNet and Zhou et al. (2018) do not adequately resolve the conflict in a video recognition context.

comments.title: Severe underspecification and errors in Algorithm 1 and related equations
comments.quote: Initialize empty set step ~~b~~ atch[]; ... return step batch[
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 suffers from multiple critical flaws: (1) The variable 'step ~~b~~ atch' suggests OCR corruption; likely intended as 'step_batch', but malformed syntax would break parsing. (2) The missing equation for d (sub-batch iterations), referenced as 'Equation 2', is absent despite being central to reproducibility. Expected formula d = floor((L - m)/n) + 1 is not provided. (3) The loop logic is flawed: after manually setting _idx_ = _idx_ - m + n, the for-loop still auto-increments, risking skipped indices or out-of-bounds access. (4) The algorithm is truncated mid-sentence ('return step batch['), leaving unclear how the final sub-batch is handled — critical for boundary conditions (drop, pad, or partial?). Together, these issues prevent unambiguous reproduction and contradict the claim of mathematical quantification.

comments.title: Ambiguity and misleading terminology in the stepped sampler mechanism
comments.quote: A novel sampler is proposed, which implements sampling in a circular and stepwise manner.
comments.explanation: The term 'circular and stepwise' is introduced without formal definition. 'Stepwise' later refers to strided iteration over sub-batches, but 'circular' is never justified — no wrapping or feedback loop is evident in the algorithm or figure. 'Repeatedly inputted' misleadingly suggests multiple exposures of identical samples in a single pass, when it merely refers to overlapping sub-batches. Similarly, 'step size' is used interchangeably with 'sub-batch size (m)', while 'step stride' refers to 'n', creating confusion as 'size' and 'stride' are distinct. These terms risk misinterpretation of the sampling mechanism, especially given high overlap (e.g., m=20, n=2 in L=25). While Figures and context partially clarify, the terminology remains imprecise and potentially misleading for implementation.

comments.title: Unjustified methodological choices: PyTorch version and training from scratch
comments.quote: The reason why we chose old version PyTorch is... the experimental effect may be better...
comments.explanation: The justification for using PyTorch 1.0.1 — that an older version 'may have better experimental effect' due to less functionality — lacks scientific basis. Modern DL frameworks maintain backward compatibility for core operations, and omitting newer features does not inherently eliminate confounders. In fact, it may introduce them (e.g., absence of modern stabilizers). Similarly, training from scratch is used to isolate the sampler effect, but this increases variance and sensitivity to optimization quirks, potentially exaggerating or distorting the stepped sampler's impact. These choices are framed as reducing interference, but may instead confound results — especially if the sampler's benefits arise indirectly from compensating for poor initialization or learning dynamics, not its design.

comments.title: Inadequate formalization and loose statistical claims
comments.quote: If the batch size and the step size are fixed, the detection effect seems to be a normal distribution of the step stride.
comments.explanation: Referring to the performance trend over stride values as a 'normal distribution' is statistically imprecise and misleading. The authors observe a unimodal pattern across five discrete strides and offer no fit or test for normality. This language risks implying probabilistic modeling where none exists. Additionally, equating 'correlation' directly with 'temporal information' without supporting proof or elaboration — beyond citing Appendix A — oversimplifies temporal dependencies in time series. While these definitions may exist in the appendix, their absence in the main text weakens the rigor of an otherwise mathematically framed argument.

comments.title: Citation discrepancy for Ebbinghaus's work
comments.quote: Hermann Ebbinghaus proposed the law of human memory and how to improve human learning in 1885. ... (Ebbinghaus, 1913)
comments.explanation: While citing the 1913 English translation of Ebbinghaus’s 1885 original is academically acceptable, the text explicitly attributes the proposal to 1885 while citing 1913, creating a misleading disconnect. The reference entry lists both dates but gives the year as 1913, which may imply the work originated then. Given the central role of Ebbinghaus’s memory experiments in motivating the 'repeated input' design, precise attribution strengthens scholarly credibility. The issue is minor but affects academic rigor in historical references.

comments.title: Grammatical error and malformed text in key explanations
comments.quote: We definite these correlations are temporal information in Appendix A.
comments.explanation: The verb 'definite' is a clear typo — the correct word is 'define'. This appears in a critical section where the authors claim to formally link 'correlations' to 'temporal information'. While the meaning is recoverable, the error introduces momentary confusion and degrades professionalism. Similarly, Algorithm 1 contains '~~b~~' artifacts likely from OCR, such as 'step ~~b~~ atch', which must be inferred as 'step_batch'. These mechanical issues, while not conceptual, undermine clarity and reproducibility — especially in core technical passages.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'circular and stepwise' sampling mechanism
comments.quote: A novel sampler is proposed, which implements sampling in a circular and stepwise manner.
comments.explanation: The term 'circular and stepwise' is introduced in the contribution section but lacks immediate formal definition. Initially, this raised concern about the precise nature of the sampling—whether 'circular' implies periodic wrapping around the dataset or some feedback loop in the batch. However, later in Section 3.3 and Figure 2, the mechanism is clarified: sub-batches within a batch partially overlap via a fixed stride, suggesting 'stepwise' refers to strided sub-batch iteration. 'Circular' remains unexplained—there is no indication in the algorithm or figure that the sampler wraps around to the beginning of the batch or dataset. While the figure and Algorithm 1 clarify the stepwise sub-batch division and stride, the use of 'circular' is not justified and does not align with the described mechanism. The authors may have intended to describe a rolling window or reuse within a batch, but 'circular' is misleading and absent from further formalization. This could confuse a reader about the sampler's structure, especially in boundary conditions.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim about test accuracy superiority
comments.quote: Meanwhile, it can maintain a higher test accuracy, compared with the traditional sampler.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the stepped sampler achieves higher test accuracy than traditional samplers, but the results in Section 4.3 contradict this. For batch size 25, the traditional sampler achieves 0.656 test accuracy versus 0.603 for the best stepped sampler (stride 2). Only with batch size 15 does the stepped sampler outperform (0.639 vs lower traditional performance). The abstract's general claim is therefore overstated: the improvement is conditional and not consistently observed. The authors correctly report the mixed outcome later, but the abstract presents an unconditional advantage. This overstatement could mislead readers into thinking the method universally improves accuracy. Given that the key contribution highlights higher accuracy, this is a questionable claim. Context partially resolves it upon reading results, but the abstract should reflect the nuanced outcome.

comments.title: Misattribution of Ebbinghaus' work to 1913
comments.quote: Hermann Ebbinghaus proposed the law of human memory and how to improve human learning in 1885. ... (Ebbinghaus, 1913)
comments.explanation: The text correctly notes Ebbinghaus' work dates from 1885, which aligns with historical fact—the forgetting curve was introduced in *Über das Gedächtnis* (1885). However, all citations for Ebbinghaus are given as (Ebbinghaus, 1913), which is an English translation of the original work. In academic context, citing the 1913 translation is acceptable, but the mismatch between the description ('proposed in 1885') and the citation year may confuse readers about the source's originality or edition. While this is common in citation practices, the juxtaposition could imply an error. However, such citations are standard (e.g., citing Marx 1967 for 1867 work), so it is not a true error. No revision needed, but mild ambiguity exists.

comments.title: Undefined variables in stepped sampler description
comments.quote: Assuming that the iteration number of the stepped sampler in a batch is d, it can be concluded from the figure: It can be deduced that, the stepped sampler iteration number per batch, d is:
comments.explanation: The passage states that 'd' can be deduced from the figure, but no equation follows—it is missing from the provided text. Given that this section leads into Algorithm 1 and refers to Equation 2, it is likely that a formula for d = floor((L - m) / n) + 1 or similar was omitted due to OCR or truncation. As written, this is a clear gap: no derivation or formula is shown, undermining the claim of quantifying the algorithm. The variables L (batch size), m (sub-batch size), and n (stride) are visually defined in Figure 2, so a careful reader could infer the intended relation. However, the absence of the equation and derivation requested in contribution (c) is a methodological shortfall. The authors promised mathematical formalization, but it is underspecified here. The issue is moderate because the algorithm might still be reproducible from Figure 2 and Algorithm 1, but the lack of explicit formula reduces clarity.

comments.title: Unverified claim about temporal information reduction via shuffling
comments.quote: We analyse that, the reason should be that the shuffle operation can reduce the correlation. According to our definition in Appendix A, the correlation is the temporal information.
comments.explanation: The authors assert that shuffling reduces temporal information because it reduces 'correlation,' per their definition in Appendix A. However, this claim is presented without proof or reference. In time series modeling, shuffling typically destroys temporal order and thus temporal dependencies—this is standard. However, equating 'correlation' directly with 'temporal information' is reductive unless formally established. In time series, temporal information includes higher-order dependencies beyond pairwise correlation. The authors may have a specific definition in Appendix A, which is not provided, so we must assume it is correctly defined there. But in the absence of that, this passage risks oversimplifying. Since they reference their own appendix for the definition, the method could be sound. The concern is mitigated by this forward reference, but the claim in isolation is underspecified. Given the paper's emphasis on formalizing temporal information mathematically, this justification feels light.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of 'repeated input' without data duplication in sampling
comments.quote: We repeatedly inputted data to the LSTM model stepwise in a batch.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'repeatedly inputted' suggests that the same data point is shown multiple times during training, which would constitute true repetition. However, from Figure 2 and the description of sub-batches with stride n, the method implements overlapping sub-batches—i.e., some frames appear in multiple sub-batches within a batch, but not repeated within the same forward pass. The data is reused across sub-batches, not 'repeated' in the sense of multiple presentations of the same sample in one sequence. The risk is that readers may interpret this as true repetition (like showing frame t five times), which could affect gradient computation differently. The actual method is more akin to a strided sliding window over the batch, not repetition per se. OCR is not at fault—the word 'repeated' is used throughout. While 'repetition' is used metaphorically (from Ebbinghaus), in technical terms, this is temporal overlap, not input repetition. The term is misleading and could affect reproducibility. However, the mechanism is visually and algorithmically clarified, so the issue is primarily semantic.

comments.title: Underspecified Algorithm 1 and missing equation
comments.quote: Equation 2 is used as the iteration number within a batch in Algorithm 1. _d_ is computed by the algorithm when _L_, _m_, _n_ is determined.
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 is referenced but not provided in the text, and Equation 2 is completely missing. This is a critical omission for reproducibility. The authors state that the algorithm implements stepped sampling after PyTorch’s sequential and batch samplers, and line 12 mentions moving the starting coordinate by n. Without Algorithm 1 or Equation 2, a reader cannot verify how d (number of sub-batch iterations) is computed. This violates the authors’ claim to 'quantify the algorithm'. While L, m, n are defined and d could be inferred as the number of positions a window of size m can slide over a batch of size L with stride n, the exact formula and handling of edge cases (e.g. incomplete sub-batches) are absent. Even if d is floored as stated, implementation details matter. This missing content renders the method underspecified, despite Figure 2 giving a partial intuition. The severity is high due to impact on reproducibility, but since the paper is under review and appendices may contain missing elements, and OCR may have dropped content, we downgrade to moderate.

comments.title: Incorrect Verb Form 'definite' Instead of 'define'
comments.quote: We definite these correlations are temporal information in Appendix A.
comments.explanation: The sentence uses 'definite' as a verb, which is grammatically incorrect—'definite' is an adjective, while the intended word is almost certainly 'define'. This is likely a typo or OCR error. Given the context—where the authors are formally linking 'correlations between knowledge' to 'temporal information' as defined in Appendix A—the intended meaning is clear. However, the error could momentarily confuse a reader into questioning whether a typographical or conceptual mistake was made. Since the claim is formally deferred to an appendix, the core technical argument isn't undermined. After checking related sections (e.g., the PAPER SUMMARY states temporal information is mathematically modeled in Appendix A), the intent is resolved. But the sentence remains poorly formed and risks misleading readers unfamiliar with the terminology.

comments.title: Unjustified Claim about PyTorch Version Choice
comments.quote: The reason why we chose old version PyTorch is, the performance of the old version may not be so powelful, however, the experimental effect may be better, since the contrast of the results may be larger. And since the old version may not have too many functions, it can focus on the factor of “repeat input”, without the interferences from other irrelevant factors.
comments.explanation: The authors justify using PyTorch 1.0.1 (2018) by claiming it enhances 'contrast' and reduces interference from other functions. However, this reasoning is scientifically questionable. Modern deep learning frameworks like PyTorch maintain backward compatibility and do not fundamentally alter core behaviors (e.g., gradient computation, optimizer steps) in ways that would confound a controlled experiment like this unless specific updated components (e.g., AMP, fused ops) were used. The claim that an older version yields 'better experimental effect' due to reduced functionality suggests a misunderstanding of reproducibility principles. While using an older version is not inherently invalid, the justification risks implying that results are contingent on obsolete software quirks rather than the proposed method. No evidence is provided that PyTorch updates introduce confounding variables, and standard practice assumes equivalent behavior across versions unless stated otherwise. This overreach could mislead readers about the robustness of the findings.

comments.title: Missing Equation for Iteration Number d
comments.quote:
It can be deduced that, the stepped sampler iteration number per batch, d is:

Equation 2 is used as the iteration number within a batch in Algorithm 1.
comments.explanation: The text states that 'Equation 2' defines d (the number of sub-batch iterations per batch), but no equation is visible in the provided passage. From context—specifically Figure 2 and the description in Section 3.4—we infer that d should be related to batch size L, sub-batch size m, and stride n. A plausible formula is d = floor((L - m) / n) + 1, which matches standard sliding-window logic. The paper summary notes that derivations appear in appendices, so the missing equation may appear there. However, in a self-contained methods section, especially one describing an algorithm, omitting the actual equation harms reproducibility. The reference to 'Equation 2' without its presence—even if defined later—is a gap in the presentation. While readers might reconstruct it, the absence still underspecifies the method in this section, particularly around boundary conditions (e.g., handling incomplete sub-batches).

comments.title: Inconsistent Claim about Test Accuracy in Figure 4
comments.quote: Nonetheless, the test accuracy score of the traditional batch sampler model (Figure 4 (a)) is slightly higher. The test accuracy of Figure 4 (a) can be 0.656, while the test accuracy of the model with stride 2 stepped sampler (Figure 4 (c)) can be 0.603.
comments.explanation: This directly contradicts a central claim in the paper summary: 'Test accuracy is higher and more stable when using the stepped sampler, especially at a batch size of 15.' Here, at batch size 25, the traditional sampler outperforms the stepped sampler. While the paper later notes improvement at batch size 15, this reversal is not adequately contextualized in the abstract or contributions. The claim in Section 1.1(a) that 'test accuracy... is much higher' with the stepped sampler is therefore misleading without qualification. The issue isn't numerical inconsistency per se—both values are plausibly reported—but rather that the paper frames the stepped sampler as universally beneficial, while results show performance dependence on hyperparameters (like batch size). The lack of emphasis on this nuance risks overstating the method's robustness. A careful reader might conclude the method is only beneficial under specific conditions, which should be explicitly acknowledged.

comments.title: Ambiguous Description of Stepped Sampler Workflow
comments.quote: the data first goes through the sequential sampler of PyTorch, then, they are processed to batches by the batch sampler. Finally, the data in each batch are divided into sub-batches with the same strides by the stepped sampler.
comments.explanation: The description suggests a pipeline: SequentialSampler → BatchSampler → SteppedSampler. But PyTorch's sampler hierarchy typically uses *one* sampler per DataLoader. A standard BatchSampler wraps another sampler (e.g., SequentialSampler) to form batches. The 'stepped sampler' appears to be a custom BatchSampler that generates overlapping sub-batches per batch index. However, the phrasing implies three samplers are chained additively, which is not standard usage and could confuse readers about implementation. The ambiguity lies in whether the 'stepped sampler' replaces or extends the batch sampler. Figure 2 and Algorithm 1 suggest it's a modified batch-level logic, but without access to the full algorithm (truncated in the passage), key details—like how data indices are generated or how the base sampler is wrapped—are missing. This underspecifies the method and could prevent reproduction.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim about test accuracy superiority of stepped sampler
comments.quote: The stepped sampler LSTM may have a higher test accuracy than the traditional sampler LSTM, in the same batch size, which can reach the value of 0.639 (Table 2).
comments.explanation: At first, this statement appears consistent with the results in smaller batch sizes (e.g., Figure 6 with batch size 15), where the stepped sampler does achieve higher test accuracy (0.639 in Table 2) than the traditional sampler (0.623). However, in the earlier part of Section 4.3, with batch size 25, the opposite is stated: the traditional sampler achieves 0.656 vs. 0.603 for the best stepped sampler. The authors do acknowledge this discrepancy ('However, LSTM with the traditional sampler whose batch size is 25 has a higher test accuracy...'), but then later present the 0.639 result as evidence that the stepped sampler 'may have higher test accuracy' without qualifying it as batch-size-dependent. This creates a misleading narrative that the stepped sampler generally improves test accuracy, when the evidence is mixed and highly parameter-sensitive. The claim would be reasonable if qualified (e.g., 'under certain hyperparameter settings'), but as written, it overgeneralizes from a subset of favorable results. While the authors correctly report individual results, the synthesis across experiments lacks nuance.

comments.title: Contradiction between jitter reduction claim and result in Figure 6(a)
comments.quote: It can be seen that, there is a large jitter when the epoch is about 100 in Figure 6 (a).
comments.explanation: The authors claim throughout the paper that the stepped sampler reduces jitter and improves stability after convergence. This is supported by qualitative observations in Figures 4 and 6, where stepped samplers show smoother curves. However, the text states there is 'a large jitter when the epoch is about 100 in Figure 6(a)'—i.e., well after training begins—while asserting that the stepped sampler eliminates such issues. This is not an error per se, but it highlights that the traditional sampler exhibits instability even during mid-to-late training, which weakens the implied novelty of the stepped sampler's stability. If jitter occurs at epoch 100, it suggests model instability beyond initial learning, which the stepped sampler helps mitigate. But the authors do not analyze why this late-stage jitter occurs or how their method suppresses it, only describing it as an observed difference. While the visual evidence supports the claim, the lack of deeper analysis (e.g., relating jitter to gradient dynamics or batch structure) leaves the explanation superficial, especially given their focus on temporal continuity and memory effects.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'step size' vs. 'step stride' terminology and values
comments.quote: From Figure 4, we consider that step stride 2 (batch size 25, step size 20, in Figure 4 (c)) is the optimal.
comments.explanation: Initially, the use of 'step size 20' alongside 'step stride 2' in the same context is confusing, because in Figure 2 and Algorithm 1, 'step size' is defined as m (sub-batch size) and 'step stride' as n (shift between sub-batches). If batch size L=25 and 'step size' m=20, then Equation 2 gives d = floor((25 - 20)/n) + 1. For n=2, d = floor(5/2)+1 = 2+1=3 sub-batch iterations per batch. While mathematically valid, the choice of m=20 (very close to L=25) with small strides like n=2 or n=1 results in high overlap (e.g., 18 out of 20 elements repeated). The term 'step size' is also ambiguous—it could be misread as synonymous with 'stride', especially since 'step' appears in 'stepped sampler'. The authors correctly use m and n in definitions, but in prose they freely mix 'step size' (meaning m) and 'step' (meaning stride). This risks confusion. Moreover, in Figure 2, m=3 and n=1 are illustrative, but in experiments m=20 or m=10 are used with L=25 or 15, pushing the design toward extreme redundancy. There's no discussion of whether such high overlap is practical or how it scales. The method is reproducible, but the naming could mislead readers about the role of m vs. n.

comments.title: Misleading implication about 'normal distribution' of detection effect over step stride
comments.quote: If the batch size and the step size are fixed, the detection effect seems to be a normal distribution of the step stride.
comments.explanation: The authors observe that performance is worse at both small and large strides and suggest the effect 'seems to be a normal distribution' over step stride. This is an imprecise statistical claim. They provide no fit, no quantitative evidence of normality, and only five stride variants (Figure 4: b–f). Observing a unimodal, symmetric pattern does not imply normality, especially with ordinal hyperparameters. In optimization, such a trend is more appropriately described as 'unimodal' or 'inverted U-shaped'. Invoking 'normal distribution' risks misleading readers into thinking the stride is a random variable or that statistical inference is being performed, which is not the case. While this is largely a rhetorical flaw, in an academic context, especially with emphasis on mathematical modeling (Section 1.1, Appendix A), such loose language undermines precision. The core observation—that there exists an optimal intermediate stride—is valid and useful, but the framing overreaches.

comments.title: Inconsistency between training from scratch and focus on sampler effect
comments.quote: Our experiment is trained from scratch. Training from scratch may decrease the test accuracy, but it can eliminate interference and focus on the stepped sampler.
comments.explanation: The authors justify training from scratch to isolate the effect of the stepped sampler, which is a valid methodological choice. However, training from scratch in video recognition (especially with UCF101) typically results in lower performance and higher variance unless carefully regularized. The authors acknowledge it may decrease test accuracy, but do not address whether this increases sensitivity to batch structure or makes convergence behavior harder to interpret. In particular, if the base model is suboptimal, the relative benefits of the stepped sampler could be exaggerated or confounded by instability. The use of older PyTorch (1.0.1) compounds this, as modern stabilization techniques (e.g., improved Adam variants, better initialization) are absent. While their goal of reducing confounders is sound, the combination of scratch training and outdated framework may introduce new confounders (e.g., optimization difficulties) that the stepped sampler indirectly mitigates—not due to 'repeated input' per se, but due to more frequent exposure compensating for poor learning dynamics. This nuance is not discussed, which could lead readers to attribute gains solely to memory-inspired design when implementation artifacts may contribute.

comments.title: Contradictory implications about shuffle's effect on temporal information
comments.quote: We analyse that, the reason should be that the shuffle operation can reduce the correlation. According to our definition in Appendix A, the correlation is the temporal information. Therefore, we consider that the shuffle operation can reduce the temporal information.
comments.explanation: The authors define correlation as temporal information (referring to Appendix A, not provided here but assumed consistent). They then argue that since shuffle reduces correlation, it reduces temporal information. However, they apply shuffle during testing and claim this makes test results 'reflect the detection effect better'. This is contradictory: if temporal information is crucial for video detection (a core claim), then reducing it via shuffling should harm performance, especially for LSTM models designed to exploit temporal dependencies. Yet they cite ShuffleNet (an image classification method) to justify shuffling improving mAP, without acknowledging that video tasks differ fundamentally from image tasks in their reliance on order. Furthermore, if they are shuffling test data, any benefit of the stepped sampler—which relies on preserving and exploiting sequence structure—may be underestimated. The authors argue that 'continuity will have a certain impact', but then test under shuffled (discontinuous) conditions. This weakens the evaluation setup: they design a method to enhance temporal learning but evaluate it on data where temporal structure is randomized. While Zhou et al. (2018) is cited to downplay shuffle’s impact on UCF101, without reproducing that result or providing ablation, it's a weak justification. The method-experiment mismatch risks invalidating the core claim.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 contains malformed variable names likely due to OCR error
comments.explanation: At first glance, the expressions 'step ~~b~~ atch' and 'step_ ~~_b_~~ _atch[]' appear to be serious notation inconsistencies or programming errors, suggesting a variable name with tildes inserted—likely from OCR corruption during PDF extraction. Given the surrounding context, particularly Figure 2 and the prose describing sub-batches, it's clear the intended variable is 'step_batch' (a reasonable name for a temporary sub-batch container). The logic of the algorithm—to fill a sub-batch of size m, output it, reset, and jump forward by stride n—is coherent if we assume the tildes are artifacts. However, the code as written would not parse due to malformed identifiers. While the authors’ intent is reconstructible, an uncorrected OCR error in a core algorithm risks reproducibility. Since this appears in the formal algorithm block, it constitutes a real risk to clarity despite being likely mechanical.

comments.title: Incorrect and potentially misleading claim about optimal step stride in batch size 25 experiments
comments.quote: From Figure 4, we consider that step stride 2 (batch size 25, step size 20, in Figure 4 (c)) is the optimal.
comments.explanation: The authors assert that stride 2 is optimal for batch size 25 based on Figure 4 and Table 1. However, Table 1 shows that the 'stride 5 stepped' sampler reaches the highest test accuracy at epoch 150 (0.593), surpassing stride 2 (0.587). While stride 2 achieves higher accuracy earlier (0.602 at epoch 120), it drops by epoch 150, whereas stride 5 increases over time. The final performance—typically the standard evaluation metric—is better for stride 5. Though the loss curve in Figure 4(c) looks smoother and converges faster, the claim of optimality is not supported when considering final accuracy. The authors appear to prioritize convergence speed and stability over final performance, but they do not explicitly justify this trade-off. This risks overclaiming support for a particular configuration, especially given their broader claim about an 'optimal step stride'.

comments.title: Contradictory claims about test accuracy under batch size 25
comments.quote: Nonetheless, the test accuracy score of the traditional batch sampler model (Figure 4 (a)) is slightly higher. The test accuracy of Figure 4 (a) can be 0.656, while the test accuracy of the model with stride 2 stepped sampler (Figure 4 (c)) can be 0.603.
comments.explanation: In Section 4.3, the authors state that the traditional sampler achieves 0.656 test accuracy while the best stepped sampler (stride 2) reaches only 0.603. However, Table 1 reports the traditional sampler's accuracy at epoch 150 as 0.635—not 0.656. Similarly, stride 2 stepped sampler is listed as 0.587, not 0.603. The value 0.656 appears in Table 1 at epoch 100, suggesting the authors may have accidentally cited a non-final accuracy. This numerical inconsistency undermines trust in the reported results: either the text misreports the numbers, or the table is incorrect. The final test accuracy comparison is crucial to claims about performance, and such a discrepancy (0.656 vs. 0.635) could mislead readers about the significance of results.

comments.title: Overstated claim of higher test accuracy for stepped sampler when evidence shows otherwise for batch size 25
comments.quote: When the batch size is 15, the test accuracy of the stepped sampler LSTM is much higher than that of the traditional sampler with the same parameters.
comments.explanation: This claim is supported in Table 2 for batch size 15: the stride 5 stepped sampler achieves 0.637 at epoch 150, outperforming the traditional sampler's 0.616. However, the same claim is made or implied for batch size 25 (e.g., in the abstract and Section 1.1), where Table 1 shows the opposite: traditional sampler achieves 0.635 vs. 0.593 for the best stepped variant. The paper does not qualify this claim to apply only to smaller batch sizes, creating a misleading overall impression. While performance may vary with batch size, the unqualified assertion that the stepped sampler 'maintains higher test accuracy' or is 'much higher' overgeneralizes from a specific case. This selective emphasis distorts the paper’s contribution.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 incorrectly omits loop continuation after stride update
comments.quote:
_idx_ = _idx − m_ + _n_ ; // move the coordinate to the next sub-batch by stride n

**end**

**end**

**end**
comments.explanation: After resetting the step_batch and updating idx to idx - m + n, the inner loop continues by incrementing idx at the top of the for-loop ('_idx_ + = 1'). But the current index has already been advanced via manual assignment. This double increment risks skipping elements or going out of bounds. For example, if m=3, n=1, and idx is at 3 when a sub-batch is filled, after setting idx = 3 - 3 + 1 = 1, the next loop iteration increments it to 2, skipping index 1. The correct behavior should be to set the next starting index and continue from there—without an additional increment. The algorithm likely assumes that idx is not automatically incremented after manual assignment, but in most programming languages, a for-loop with idx++ will override this. The pseudocode is underspecified: it does not clarify whether idx is managed manually or by the loop construct. This ambiguity makes the algorithm unreproducible without additional assumptions.

comments.title: Missing derivation of sub-batch iteration count (Equation 2 not provided)
comments.quote:
It can be deduced that, the stepped sampler iteration number per batch, d is:

Equation 2 is used as the iteration number within a batch in Algorithm 1.
comments.explanation: The text references 'Equation 2' as the formula for the number of iterations d per batch, but no equation is visible in the provided passage. Given the variables L (batch size), m (sub-batch size), and n (stride), the expected formula would be something like d = floor((L - m) / n) + 1, akin to convolutional sliding windows. Its absence means readers cannot verify the coverage or overlap properties of the stepped sampling. While this might appear in Appendix A or B, the main method section relies on it for correctness (e.g., ensuring d is integer). The lack of this equation in the method description is a significant underspecification, especially as it's cited as being used in Algorithm 1.

comments.title: Ambiguity in sub-batch construction algorithm leads to coverage and boundary issues
comments.explanation: The algorithm attempts to build sub-batches by iterating through indices 1 to L, but after outputting a sub-batch of size m, it resets and jumps idx to idx - m + n. However, the outer loop is a standard for-loop from 1 to L, which assumes sequential, uninterrupted iteration. Manually modifying idx within the loop disrupts this structure. Moreover, the loop may run beyond L or skip elements, especially when n < m. For example, if L=5, m=3, n=1, the first sub-batch uses indices 1,2,3; then idx becomes 1 (3-3+1), and after increment becomes 2, producing sub-batch 2,3,4; then idx becomes 2 (4-3+1=2), then 3, producing 3,4,5; then 3 (5-3+1=3), then 4—now idx=4 > L? It's unclear. The algorithm doesn't define how many sub-batches are generated or whether they fit cleanly in L. This underspecification makes it impossible to reproduce the method without heuristic fixes.

comments.title: Misleading interpretation of shuffle reducing temporal information
comments.quote: We analyse that, the reason should be that the shuffle operation can reduce the correlation. According to our definition in Appendix A, the correlation is the temporal information. Therefore, we consider that the shuffle operation can reduce the temporal information.
comments.explanation: The authors assume that shuffling test data reduces temporal information, which they equate with correlation. However, in video recognition, temporal order is essential for motion and action modeling. Shuffling at the batch level (not frame level) typically only changes sample ordering across batches, not within a sequence. If the input to LSTM is a sequence of frames, and frames within a video are preserved in order (as they should be), then shuffling across videos does not disrupt intra-video temporal information. The model still sees frames in sequence. Therefore, shuffling data loaders does not 'reduce temporal information' in any meaningful way for LSTMs—unless frames are shuffled within videos, which the authors do not claim. This misattribution weakens their conceptual argument that stepped sampling enhances temporal modeling, as the baseline temporal structure is intact regardless of shuffle. The reasoning appears flawed or based on a misunderstanding of how data loaders work.

comments.title: Overstatement of Test Accuracy Superiority Despite Contradictory Evidence
comments.quote: Compared with the traditional sampler, the stepped sampler LSTM has a faster learning effect, and has a higher test accuracy under certain parameters. [...] we believe that is why not all the stepped sampler LSTM’s test accuracy is higher than the traditional sampler.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the claim that the stepped sampler achieves 'higher test accuracy under certain parameters' appears consistent with the paper's findings, especially given the discussion in Section 4.3 about improved performance at smaller batch sizes (e.g., 15). However, the phrasing risks overgeneralization, as Figure 4 clearly shows that for batch size 25 — the first and most detailed experimental condition — the traditional sampler achieves a higher test accuracy (0.656) than even the best stepped sampler variant (0.603). The authors later acknowledge that not all stepped sampler variants outperform the baseline, which weakens the initial assertion that it 'has a higher test accuracy under certain parameters' without further qualification. A more precise claim would reference specific configurations (e.g., 'at batch size 15'), but as written, the statement presents an isolated positive result as a broader trend. While some cases do show improvement, the lack of specificity could mislead a reader into thinking the method consistently enhances accuracy, which the data does not support. This is not a fatal flaw, but it does overstate the empirical support.

comments.title: Ambiguous Reference to 'Human Learning Methods' Without Clear Definition
comments.quote: We try to use some human learning methods to study artificial neural networks. [...] The attention mechanism (Vaswani et al., 2017) may be also inspired by human learning methods. Transfer learning (Bozinovski & Fulgosi, 1976) is using old knowledge to learn new knowledge, which may be inspired by human learning methods either.
comments.explanation: The passage attempts to position the stepped sampler as part of a broader pattern of human-inspired machine learning, citing attention and transfer learning as precedents. However, the term 'human learning methods' is used loosely and without formal definition. Attention mechanisms are indeed inspired by cognitive processes, but transfer learning — particularly as cited — predates modern neural networks and was not originally motivated by cognitive science. The 1976 reference is to a perceptron study with no discussion of human learning. While the authors likely mean 'human learning strategies' in a conceptual sense (e.g., review, reuse of knowledge), equating transfer learning directly with human learning without qualification risks misrepresentation. The concern is not that the analogy is invalid, but that it is asserted too confidently without distinguishing between direct bio-inspiration and post-hoc analogy. The context partially resolves this by framing these as 'may be inspired', which softens the claim, but the implication remains that prior works explicitly drew from human cognition, which is not always the case.

comments.title: Inconsistent Interpretation of Shuffle Operation's Effect on Temporal Information
comments.quote: We analyse that, the reason should be that the shuffle operation can reduce the correlation. According to our definition in Appendix A, the correlation is the temporal information. Therefore, we consider that the shuffle operation can reduce the temporal information.
comments.explanation: The authors define temporal information as frame correlation (via Appendix A), and correctly note that shuffling reduces sequential correlation. However, they then argue that because test data is shuffled, 'there should be less temporal information among the data, the test results may reflect the detection effect better.' This is logically problematic: if the model is designed to leverage temporal information (as claimed throughout), and shuffling removes that information, then test performance under shuffling actually underestimates the model's capacity to use temporal structure. The authors appear to treat reduced temporal information as beneficial for evaluation, but this contradicts their core premise that enhanced temporal modeling improves detection. A fairer interpretation would be that shuffling controls for spurious correlations or dataset bias, but it does not make evaluation 'better' in assessing temporal modeling. The concern is not with shuffling itself — it's standard practice — but with the justification that reducing temporal information improves evaluation, which undermines their own argument about the importance of temporal fusion. Context does not resolve this; it's a conceptual tension between methodology and claim.

comments.title: Underspecified Sub-Batch Handling at Batch Boundaries
comments.quote: return step batch[
comments.explanation: The algorithm snippet in Algorithm 1 is cut off mid-sentence ('return step batch['), likely due to OCR or formatting truncation. While this may appear to be a mere artifact, it raises concerns about the completeness of the method description. Specifically, it's unclear how the algorithm handles the final sub-batch when the remaining data in a batch does not reach size m (the step size). Does it pad, drop, or output a shorter sub-batch? This boundary condition is critical because uneven sub-batch sizes could affect training dynamics, gradient magnitudes, or convergence. The paper assumes L > m ≥ n and uses floor rounding for the number of iterations d, suggesting incomplete sub-batches are discarded. But this is never explicitly stated. Other parts of the paper assume full sub-batches (e.g., Figure 2 uses exact divisions), so the omission could mislead reproducibility. While experienced practitioners might infer reasonable defaults, the method section should specify this for clarity. The issue persists even under plausible symbol correction, as the logic gap remains.

comments.title: Misattribution of Ebbinghaus’s Work to 1913 Publication
comments.quote: Hermann Ebbinghaus. Memory: A contribution to experimental psychology, 1885. _New York: Teachers College, Columbia University_ , 1913.
comments.explanation: This is a bibliographic error that affects scholarly accuracy. Ebbinghaus's original work was published in German in 1885; the 1913 edition cited is an English translation. Throughout the paper, the authors cite 'Ebbinghaus, 1913' when discussing claims made in 1885. While common in citations, this can be misleading if not clarified. More seriously, the abstract states, 'Hermann Ebbinghaus proposed the law of human memory and how to improve human learning in 1885' while citing 'Ebbinghaus, 1913', which creates a disconnect. The reference itself lists both dates but formats the year as 1913, suggesting the authors may not have distinguished between original and translated editions. This is not technically an error in logic or method, but it weakens academic rigor, especially in a paper building its motivation on historical ideas. Given the central role of Ebbinghaus, precision in citation enhances credibility.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: While the paper addresses an interesting intersection between cognitive psychology—specifically Ebbinghaus’s notions of spaced repetition—and machine learning optimization, it suffers from significant conceptual and stylistic shortcomings that undermine its academic rigor. The core premise, which suggests that repeatedly feeding data to an LSTM using a "stepped sampler" mimics human memory, lacks a formal mathematical framework connecting biological cognitive theories to gradient-based optimization dynamics. The writing is frequently informal (e.g., using phrases like "we courageously assume"), and the literature review, while broad, fails to critically integrate existing research on curriculum learning or optimization-based sampling, opting instead for a cursory survey of unrelated techniques. To meet the standard of top-tier venues like ICLR, the authors must shift from speculative analogies to a more robust, empirical investigation that clearly separates the hypothesized "biomimetic" benefits from the algorithmic impact of the proposed sampling strategy on loss landscape exploration.

comments.title: Missing Mathematical Formulation and Definitions
comments.quote: As shown in Figure 2, assuming that the iteration number of the stepped sampler in a batch is d, it can be concluded from the figure: It can be deduced that, the stepped sampler iteration number per batch, d is: Equation 2 is used as the iteration number within a batch in Algorithm 1.
comments.explanation: Key mathematical formulations, specifically the calculation for the sub-batch iteration number 'd', are missing from the text. This renders the primary methodological contribution, the 'stepped sampler', underspecified and non-reproducible.

comments.title: Logical Inconsistency regarding Temporal Information and Shuffle Operations
comments.quote: We analyse that, the reason should be that the shuffle operation can reduce the correlation. According to our definition in Appendix A, the correlation is the temporal information. ... we think there would be no disadvantages for us to use the shuffle operation when testing.
comments.explanation: The authors simultaneously claim their method exploits 'temporal information' while justifying the use of shuffle operations (during both training and evaluation) that destroy the very temporal signals the architecture is designed to model. The failure to reconcile how their method preserves temporal dependencies while using shuffle operations—essential for an LSTM-based video recognition task—undermines the validity of their evaluation.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated Statistical Claims regarding 'Normal Distribution' Trends
comments.quote: If the batch size and the step size are fixed, the detection effect seems to be a normal distribution of the step stride.
comments.explanation: The authors make a strong statistical claim that their detection accuracy follows a 'normal distribution' relative to the step stride. This is unsubstantiated by the provided data, which consists only of a few discrete experimental points. The paper fails to provide the necessary statistical rigor (e.g., variance or goodness-of-fit analysis) to justify this claim.

comments.title: Conflation of Performance Metrics and Data Characteristics
comments.quote: We try to apply the mathematical equations to our experimental results, and analyze that the test accuracy in the experiment is the temporal information between video frames.
comments.explanation: The authors equate 'test accuracy' (a performance metric) with 'temporal information' (a data characteristic) without a rigorous definition or proof, creating a logically tenuous analytical claim.

comments.title: Inconsistent Reporting of Experimental Efficacy
comments.quote: The test accuracy score of Figure 6 (c) is higher than Figure 6 (a), which denotes that, the stepped sampler LSTM may have higher test accuracy than the traditional sampler LSTM, when the batch size is 15
comments.explanation: The authors characterize their model as superior despite inconsistent empirical results; the traditional sampler outperforms the proposed method in certain hyperparameter configurations (e.g., batch size 25). The paper lacks an objective discussion of this trade-off between convergence speed and final accuracy, leading to misleading claims regarding the model's superiority.

comments.title: Ambiguous and Erroneous Algorithm Notation
comments.quote: Initialize empty set step ~~b~~ atch[]; ... for idx = 1, 2, ..., L do ... output the idx-th item batch[idx] into step batch[]; idx += 1; if len(step_ ~~b_~~ atch[]) == m then
